nativeRange.habitat: Shallow clear coastal and outer reef crests with rich coral growth, typically to about 15 m depth; depth range 1–28 m

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on marine and blue-green algae (Spirulina, Chlorella) growing on live rock. Has been documented accepting frozen and dried algae-based foods, and may opportunistically take small meaty items such as brine shrimp or mysid shrimp. Specimens that lack sufficient algae in the aquarium tend to decline; supplemental algae-based foods are generally advised.

avoidWith: fish that may intimidate small perching species

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 2867x1911. Tack-sharp full-body side view against a clean teal background; clearly shows the diagnostic dark lateral line breaking into a row of square blotches toward the tail, pale pinkish belly and eye cirri. Most striking pick.
Diego Delso, 2720x1813. Sharp full-body view (facing right) perched on reef, same diagnostic square-blotch lateral line and pink belly; different angle for variety.
Francois Libert, 1000x720. Different photographer/individual on live rock; sharp and correctly ID'd showing the lined pattern and blotches, though background is busier. Adds source variety.

summary: The Linear Blenny is a small, peaceful herbivore from Indo-Pacific reefs, generally considered straightforward to keep in a reef aquarium with ample live rock for grazing and perching. It is broadly regarded as reef-safe, though occasional SPS coral nipping has been reported. A tightly sealed lid is essential, as this species is prone to jumping.
careNotes:
The Linear Blenny (Ecsenius lineatus) is generally considered a good fit for beginners to reef-keeping. It tends to spend most of its time perching on rocks or darting between rubble, grazing on algae growing across live rock surfaces. A tight-fitting lid or sealed hood is strongly advised — this species is widely reported to be a persistent jumper and will exploit any gap in the aquarium cover.

These fish have been reported to occasionally nip at the polyps or bases of SPS corals (small-polyp stony corals such as Acropora and Montipora), particularly in lightly stocked or coral-dominated systems. Adding one to an established SPS reef is generally approached with caution.

This species tends to be aggressive toward its own kind: keeping only one per aquarium is generally advised unless a mated pair can be established. Multiple males or similarly colored blennies in the same tank tend to result in persistent chasing. Similarly shaped blennies from other species may also trigger territorial responses.

This species should not be housed with aggressive tankmates that may bully it into permanent hiding, which can lead to starvation. Diet is primarily algae-based, and specimens that lack sufficient natural algae in the aquarium have been known to decline without supplemental algae-based foods.
breedingNotes: The Linear Blenny is oviparous (egg-laying) and exhibits distinct pairing behavior. Eggs are demersal and adhesive, attached to the substrate via a filamentous pad. Captive breeding has not been widely documented in the hobby.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (confirmed 9.0 cm TL), native range (Yemen to NW Australia, Philippines, Japan), temperature range (24.6–29.3°C wild data). FishBase temperature max is higher than hobby sources (25.6–26.7°C); averaged across sources yields 23–28°C practical range.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–80°F / 22–26.7°C), tank size (30 gal), reef-safe caveats (SPS coral nipping), and conspecific aggression. Consistent with primary source.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Confirmed escape risk (aquarium must be covered), diet notes (small crustaceans opportunistically taken), substrate preference (rubble and gravel). Also noted this species lacks teeth — food must be appropriately sized.

fish.breedingNotes: The Linear Blenny is oviparous with distinct pairing behavior. Eggs are demersal and adhesive. Captive breeding has not been widely documented in the hobby.
